text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Response Assessment Scan -- Tagrisso Therapy, Approximately 9 Months

CLINICAL INDICATION:
62-year-old female with known Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma harboring an EGFR L858R (exon 21) activating mutation. Patient has been receiving osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line definitive systemic therapy. This examination is obtained for interval response assessment at approximately the 9-month mark. Prior imaging available for comparison includes CT chest/abdomen/pelvis from approximately 3 months after osi initiation (primary mass measured 2.1 cm at that time) as well as the pre-treatment staging CT (primary mass 4.2 cm). PET-CT from initial staging also reviewed.

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the intravenous administration of 90 mL of Omnipaque 350 contrast. Imaging was acquired during the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, with a dedicated lung window reconstruction for the chest. Axial, coronal, and sagittal multiplanar reformats reviewed. Slice thickness 1.25 mm reconstructed from 5 mm contiguous acquisition.

COMPARISON:
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: approximately 3 months after Tagrisso initiation (primary mass 2.1 cm)
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast: pre-treatment staging (primary mass 4.2 cm)

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The previously described left upper lobe spiculated mass has continued to decrease in size. On this examination, the mass measures approximately 1.5 cm in its greatest axial dimension (previously 2.1 cm at the 3-month scan; 4.2 cm at baseline staging), reflecting a cumulative reduction of approximately 64% from the pre-treatment measurement. The lesion retains some residual irregular spiculation along its margins, but there is conspicuous interval shrinkage of the associated desmoplastic reaction and surrounding ground-glass haziness that was present on prior examinations. The lesion appears largely solid with partial clearing of the surrounding parenchymal opacity. No cavitation is identified within the lesion. No new pulmonary nodules are identified in the left lung. The right lung is clear. No new nodular foci suspicious for pulmonary metastases are seen bilaterally. The central airways, including the trachea, carina, right and left main bronchi, and lobar bronchi, are patent. The left upper lobe bronchus is not endobronchially compromised. No endobronchial lesion is identified.

Mediastinum:
There has been continued interval reduction in mediastinal adenopathy compared with the 3-month follow-up study. Previously enlarged lymph nodes in the aortopulmonary window (station 5/6) and left lower paratracheal station (station 4L) have continued to regress and now appear at or near the upper limits of normal size -- the largest residual node in the aortopulmonary window measures approximately 9 mm in short-axis dimension. These nodes are no longer conspicuously enlarged by size criteria, though they remain visible. No new mediastinal adenopathy is identified. There is no mediastinal widening or vascular encasement.

Hilum:
The left hilum, which showed hilar lymphadenopathy at staging, continues to normalize in appearance. No discrete hilar mass or abnormal lymphadenopathy is identified bilaterally on this examination.

Left Supraclavicular Region (imaged on superior thoracic cuts):
Prior left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, which was near-resolved at the 3-month response scan, is again not identified as a discrete abnormally enlarged node on this examination. The soft tissue of the left supraclavicular fossa appears unremarkable.

Pleura and Pericardium:
No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion. No pleural-based thickening or nodularity is identified.

Chest Wall:
No suspicious chest wall lesions. Post-biopsy changes are not conspicuous at this interval. Soft tissues are unremarkable.

Cardiovascular:
The heart is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with no dissection or aneurysm identified. The main pulmonary artery is not enlarged. No filling defects are identified within the central or segmental pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary embolism on this examination (noting that timing was not optimized for PE protocol).

Bones (thoracic):
No sclerotic or lytic osseous lesions are identified in the visible thoracic spine, sternum, or rib cage to suggest skeletal metastatic disease. Mild degenerative disc disease is present in the thoracic spine, unchanged.

---

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. Hepatic parenchymal attenuation is homogeneous. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. No biliary ductal dilatation. The portal vein is patent.

Spleen:
Normal in size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas:
The pancreas enhances homogeneously. No ductal dilatation or pancreatic mass.

Adrenal Glands:
Both adrenal glands appear normal. No adrenal nodule or adrenal mass is identified bilaterally. This is an important negative finding in the context of known lung adenocarcinoma; the adrenal glands appear within normal limits on this examination.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis. A small, well-circumscribed, hypodense left renal cyst measuring approximately 1.3 cm is noted, consistent with a simple cyst (Bosniak I). No solid renal mass.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes:
No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy is identified. No para-aortic lymph node enlargement.

Gastrointestinal Tract:
The visualized small and large bowel demonstrate normal caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction or wall thickening. The appendix is not definitively visualized but there is no periappendiceal inflammatory change.

Pelvis:
The urinary bladder is adequately distended and wall is smooth. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid. The uterus and ovaries appear atrophic and unremarkable for the patient's age.

Bones (abdominal and pelvic):
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions are identified within the visualized lumbar spine, sacrum, or pelvis. Mild multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease with endplate changes, unchanged and age-appropriate. No pathologic fracture.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. CONTINUED SUBSTANTIAL REDUCTION of the left upper lobe primary lung adenocarcinoma, now measuring approximately 1.5 cm -- representing a cumulative decrease of approximately 64% from the pre-treatment baseline measurement of 4.2 cm and further reduction from the 2.1 cm measured at the 3-month assessment. This degree of response is consistent with an ongoing and deepening treatment response to osi.

2. MINIMAL RESIDUAL MEDIASTINAL ADENOPATHY -- previously enlarged lymph nodes at stations 4L and 5/6 have continued to decrease, with the largest residual node now at the upper limits of normal size. This reflects continued nodal response compared with staging imaging.

3. NO EVIDENCE OF LEFT SUPRACLAVICULAR LYMPHADENOPATHY -- previously identified supraclavicular nodal disease, which was near-resolved at 3 months, is not identified on this examination.

4. NO NEW PULMONARY NODULES, PLEURAL EFFUSION, OR PLEURAL-BASED DISEASE.

5. NO EVIDENCE OF DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE in the abdomen or pelvis -- specifically, no hepatic, adrenal, retroperitoneal nodal, or skeletal metastatic lesions are identified. Incidental simple left renal cyst as described.

Overall, these findings reflect continued and deepening locoregional and nodal treatment response to systemic osi therapy at the 9-month interval, without evidence of distant visceral metastatic spread at this time.

Reviewing Oncologist: [Thoracic Oncology Attending]
Technologist: [CT Tech Initials]
